facts_disclosed: Father was journalist, family relocated from Cali at age 8

turns.content: do you know anything about dealing with complicated feelings about a parent's career choices? specifically when their choices shaped your whole life in ways you're still unpacking

That's a really common but deeply personal thing to work through. A lot of people find that their parents' career decisions — the moves, the sacrifices, the absences, the values passed down — end up being the invisible architecture of their own lives.

Can you tell me more about what you mean? Are you talking about choices they made that affected the family, choices that influenced your own career path, or something else entirely?

both I guess. my dad was a journalist. in cali. he covered things that made powerful people uncomfortable and one night someone slipped a threat under our door and that was it. we left. I was 8. barely remember packing a bag, I remember my mom's face though. we moved to bogotá and he never worked in journalism again

That's an incredibly heavy thing for an 8-year-old to experience, even if you didn't fully understand it at the time. A sudden uprooting like that — the fear, the secrecy, watching your parents' world collapse overnight — that leaves deep marks.

And your father walking away from journalism entirely... that's its own kind of loss. Not just for him, but for you watching it happen. Seeing a parent give up something that defined them.

Can I ask — when you say you're still unpacking it, what does that look like for you now? Is it that you're processing the fear and disruption of that night, or more about how his choice to leave journalism (or his choice to do the work that caused the threat) shaped who you became?

the second one mostly. because here's the thing. I became a journalist too. same kind of journalism. corruption, power, the things nobody wants you to say out loud. and sometimes I wonder if I did that because I saw what it cost him to stop, how something in him just went quiet after we left cali, and I thought someone had to keep going. or maybe I'm just wired the same way he was. I don't know

That's one of those questions that probably doesn't have a clean answer — and maybe doesn't need one. Both things can be true simultaneously. You can be wired for this work AND have been shaped by watching your father's silence after he stopped. Those aren't competing explanations; they're layers of the same story.

What strikes me is the way you describe it — "something in him just went quiet." That's not anger at his choice. That's grief for what it did to him. You saw the cost of stopping, not just the cost of doing the work.

Do you ever talk to him about this? About the fact that you followed him into the same career he had to leave?

we don't talk about it directly. colombian families are good at talking around things, you know? he knows what I do. he reads everything I publish. my mom told me he prints out my articles which is the most my-dad thing I can imagine because who prints articles in 2024. but we've never sat down and said the words

The printing thing is beautiful and heartbreaking at the same time. He's following every word you write — he's proud, he's engaged, he's probably terrified — but the conversation about what it all means stays unspoken.

That's very Colombian, yes, but it's also very human. Sometimes the weight of what needs to be said feels like it would break something open that both people have carefully held together for years.

Here's what I think might be worth considering: you don't necessarily need the big dramatic conversation. Sometimes the real communication is already happening — he prints your articles, you keep doing the work. You already understand each other in the ways that matter.

But if there's a part of you that needs to hear him say something specific — whether it's "I'm proud of you" or "I'm scared for you" or "I understand why you do this" — that need is worth honoring. Even if the conversation is small and imperfect.

Does he ever express worry about your safety, even indirectly?

indirectly yes. he asks me if I'm being careful. every time I publish something big he calls and the first thing he says is "estás bien?" not "great article" or "I read it" just are you okay. and I always say yes and we both know what we're really talking about

"Estás bien?" — that's the whole conversation compressed into two words. He's asking if what happened to him is happening to you. And your "yes" is telling him you're still safe, still standing, still going.

That exchange IS the conversation you think you've never had. It's just happening in the shorthand that families develop around their deepest fears.

I want to point out something: you said something in him went quiet when he stopped reporting. But he didn't go fully quiet, did he? He prints your articles. He calls after every big story. In some way, he's still in the fight — through you. That's not a burden for you to carry, but it might explain why this feels so layered.

The complicated feelings you mentioned at the start — I think part of it might be that you're holding two roles at once. You're the journalist doing the work, AND you're the daughter who watched what this work can cost. You know both sides of it intimately.

Is that close to what you're feeling?

yeah. that's exactly it. I carry both. the drive to do it and the knowledge of what it can take from you. some days those two things coexist fine and other days they're at war inside me and I just sit there staring at my screen at 3am not sure which voice to listen to

That internal tension — the drive and the fear sitting side by side — is not a problem to be solved. It's actually what makes you good at this work. Journalists who feel no fear get reckless. Journalists who feel only fear stop. You're living in the space between, and that takes immense strength even when it feels like paralysis at 3am.

A few things that might help on the hard nights:

- **Name which voice is louder that night.** Just identifying "tonight it's fear" or "tonight it's the drive" can reduce the feeling of being pulled apart. You don't have to resolve it, just see it clearly.

- **Separate your father's story from yours.** His story had a specific threat, a specific night, a specific outcome. Your story is still being written. You are not replaying his life — you're living your own, informed by his but not bound to repeat it.

- **Consider writing about it.** Not for publication. Just for yourself. You clearly have the language for this — the way you describe his silence, the printing of articles, the "estás bien" calls — that's not just feeling, that's insight. Getting it out of your head and onto a page might give you some distance from it.

Have you ever talked about this with a therapist or anyone outside the family?

I see a therapist yeah. we talk about a lot of things. this comes up. she says I have a pattern of carrying other people's unfinished stories which is such a therapist thing to say but she's probably right

Ha — it IS such a therapist thing to say, but she's onto something real. "Carrying other people's unfinished stories" is both a psychological pattern and literally your job description as an investigative journalist. You finish the stories that powerful people want to stay unfinished. That's not a coincidence.

The fact that you're in therapy and aware of the pattern is huge. A lot of people carry this stuff without ever naming it. You're naming it, you're sitting with it, and you're still doing the work. That's not unresolved — that's living with complexity.

One last thought: your dad stopped, and something in him went quiet. But you didn't stop, and you're anything but quiet. Whatever he couldn't finish, you're not obligated to finish it FOR him — but the fact that you chose this path on your own terms, fully aware of the cost, means something different than what he went through. He was forced out. You walked in with your eyes open. That's not carrying his unfinished story. That's writing your own.
okay you just made me cry in a café which is very inconvenient but. thank you. I needed someone to say that. that it's my story not his. I know it intellectually but hearing it feels different

Knowing something and feeling it land are two completely different things. And crying in a café is very on-brand for a writer, so you're fine.

You're doing important work, and you're doing it as yourself — not as your father's proxy, not as a tribute, not as a correction of his story. You do it because you're wired for it and because it matters. The fact that he's reading every word and printing them out? That's not him living through you. That's him seeing his daughter do something extraordinary.

Whenever the 3am voice gets loud, you know where to find me. Take care of yourself, Valentina.
